sections.heading: The Results
sections.text: Jake Candler came out swinging with a two-song strategy — submitting Good Lookin' by Dixon Dallas AND Bad Luck by Zach Top — and somehow the group rewarded him for both, handing him 18 points and a Week 1 lead that he absolutely did not earn through humility. Good Lookin' took Song of the Week with 12 points, which is either a genuine endorsement of Dixon Dallas or a referendum on how easy this crowd is to please with a cowboy hat and a mustache.

sections.heading: The Chaos
sections.text: Kennon Stockwell had the most democratic song of the week — Fuck You Bitch by Wheeler Walker Jr. received votes from 7 unique people, more than any other song. Make of that what you will about the emotional state of this group. Kennon also submitted Everybody Needs Love by Drive-By Truckers in what can only be described as an emotional range that should concern the people closest to him.

sections.heading: The Wall of Shame
sections.text: Giles Geddes, Kevin Doran, and Will Balch all forgot to vote — or perhaps they were too busy going through their own divorces — and left a combined 21 points on the table. The Missed Points Board does not forget. It does not forgive. Henry Loong submitted Before He Cheats by Carrie Underwood and received zero points for it. Zero. Carrie Underwood has a Grammy. Multiple Grammys. Think about what you've done.

sections.heading: The Lesson
sections.text: Brooks Keuster submitted two genuinely good songs, got 0 points for both because he didn't vote, and is currently tied for last place with people who submitted nothing. Rough week, Brooks. See you all for Week 2. Vote or your songs die in silence.

rounds.name: Songs to Listen to While Daily Driving a Hearse on a Beautiful Day
rounds.description: It’s a perfect blue-sky day, windows down, light breeze—and you’re cruising in a hearse like it’s just another commute. There’s something weirdly peaceful about it, but also just off enough to make people do a double take at stoplights. This is the soundtrack for that contrast: bright, smooth, almost carefree music with an undercurrent of irony. Lean 70s soft rock / yacht rock / breezy indie / something more on brand for a hearse owner—something that feels like a Sunday drive, just… not in the vehicle anyone expected.

sections.heading: The Winner
sections.text: Mills T. understood the assignment better than anyone. Wakin on a Pretty Day by Kurt Vile took Song of the Week with 13 points and 7 unique voters — a dreamy, sun-drenched, 9-minute sprawl that sounds exactly like what you'd hear leaking out of a hearse doing 5 under the speed limit on a back road through Decatur. Mills went from 7th place last week to winning the whole round. That's a +13 point swing, which is the kind of momentum that makes Jake nervous.

sections.heading: Song Commentary
sections.text: Kennon submitted Jesus Was Way Cool by King Missile and got 12 points for it. If you don't know this song, it is exactly what it sounds like. It is a spoken word piece about Jesus being way cool. It is perfect for a hearse. Kennon gets it. Jake is still in first place overall at 32 points, but the gap is closing. Thunder Island by Jay Ferguson was the right vibe but only pulled 8 points.

sections.heading: The Debuts
sections.text: Hunter A. was a ghost in Week 1 — no submission, no votes, invisible. This week he submitted The Funeral by Band of Horses and Where'd All the Time Go? by Dr. Dog, pulled 15 combined points, and landed 3rd for the week. That's the single best debut performance of the season. Giles G. had a similar resurrection. Left 7 points on the table last week by not voting. This week he submitted Velvet Underground and pulled 13 points. Voting works, Giles.

sections.heading: The Fallers
sections.text: Jack A. fell off a cliff. From 9 points and a top-4 finish in Week 1 to 3 points in Week 2. That's a -6 point swing, the worst in the league. The Wall of Shame is empty this week — everyone who submitted also voted. The threat of public accountability appears to be working. But only 16 of 25 members submitted. The hearse has room.

rounds.name: Songs to Listen to When You Realize You Just Lost Your Second Adult Tooth of the Year and it Isn’t Even April Yet
rounds.description: You’re at the sink again, staring at something that absolutely shouldn’t have come out, doing the math and realizing this is the second time—and it’s not even April. This isn’t a fluke anymore, it’s a pattern. There’s a quiet, creeping dread that your body is starting to keep score, and you’re losing. This is the soundtrack for that moment where denial starts slipping into acceptance—uneasy, slightly unhinged, and darker than you want to admit. Lean moody indie, slow-burn alt rock, or stripped-down acoustic from the 2000s–2010s—songs that feel like something’s wrong, even if no one’s saying it out loud.

sections.heading: Scouting Report (written blind)
sections.text:
Who let the youth pastor in? Someone submitted Awake and Alive by Skillet AND Blow Me Away by Breaking Benjamin. These are the songs that play during the slow-motion montage in a Christian action film. Someone has a Monster Energy sticker on their laptop.

Someone submitted Creep by Radiohead. In 2026. This is the "I have a great personality" of song submissions.

Cotton Eye Joe — where did it come from? Where did it go? Where did it come from, Cotton Eye Tooth?

Hammer Smashed Face by Cannibal Corpse. The theme said "lost a tooth," not "had your skull caved in by a medieval weapon."

A 12-minute of Montreal deep cut submitted to a group that gave Dixon Dallas Song of the Week. This person was going to get zero votes and feel superior about it.

sections.heading: The Reveals
sections.text:
Emory L. detonated. First appearance of the season: 23 points. That's the highest single-week score anyone has posted. Hurt by Johnny Cash (13 pts) and Pulling Teeth by Green Day (10 pts) — one universally respected pick and one perfect title-to-theme hit. Emory showed up, chose violence, and left.

Coates C. walked in and won Song of the Week. Grillz by Nelly — 14 points, 8 voters. The new guy. First round. Coates just has it.

sections.heading: The Standings Shift
sections.text:
Mills T. is your new season leader at 42 points. Through The Wire by Kanye pulled 11 points — the most thematically perfect pick on the playlist (Kanye recorded it with his jaw wired shut).

Dray E. climbed to 2nd overall with 17 points. Widespread Panic's All Time Low and Warren Zevon's My Shit's Fucked Up are both deep cuts that people actually listened to. But All Time Low got 9 voters and only 9 points — the textbook snub. Everyone acknowledged it, nobody committed.

Jake C. posted a zero. Didn't submit. The Week 1 champion fell from 1st to 4th in one round.

sections.heading: The Unmasking
sections.text:
The youth pastor was Hilton H. Skillet AND Breaking Benjamin. Combined 1 point. Hilton, I'm not mad, I'm just disappointed.

Jack A. submitted Cannibal Corpse. Hammer Smashed Face got zero votes, because of course it did.

TR C. submitted Creep. The identity of the Creep submitter is now public record.

sections.heading: The Wall of Shame
sections.text: Will B., Henry L., and Brooks K. all submitted but didn't vote. Brooks submitted I Hate Myself And Want To Die by Nirvana — 9 points from 9 voters — and lost all of it. Three rounds in a row with zero credited points. Brooks. Please.

sections.heading: Prediction Accuracy
sections.text: Submitter guesses: 1 for 6. Only got Schuyler M. and Where Is My Mind. Vote predictions: had Through The Wire winning (it came 3rd), Grillz at 4th (it won). The model needs work. The dental humor was stronger than expected.

This is the soundtrack for chaotic productivity with a survival instinct kicking in. Lean high-energy funk, fast-paced indie, or jittery electronic—songs that feel twitchy, driven, and just a little unhinged.

sections.heading: Scouting Report (written blind)
sections.text:
The prompt wasn't nuts. It was you're a squirrel and it's time to gather those nuts — which is a completely different prompt. It's not crazy. It's not innuendo. It's autumn. It's work. It's a small frantic animal preparing for winter because winter is coming and nobody else is going to feed it.

Roughly half the playlist read the first word and stopped. The other half read the whole sentence. The scoreboard should sort them out.

The squirrels (read the whole sentence): Stick Season by Noah Kahan. The single best theme-fit on the playlist. New England fall. Bare trees. Paired with I Remember Everything from the same submitter. This is a two-song thesis.

Working Man Blues and Milkman from the same submitter. A country standard about a man whose entire identity is providing, paired with a funk song about going door-to-door delivering food. The squirrel prompt through a working-class lens.

Copperhead Road. A guy running moonshine because that's what his family always did to survive. That's rural gathering for winter, told by a human with a rifle.

A-Punk by Vampire Weekend. Frantic drums, winter imagery, Hudson River filled with snow. Stealth theme fit.

The keyword skimmers read "nuts," submitted a song with "nut" in the title, and closed the laptop: Gimmie That Nutt, Hot Nuts, Nut Nut Nut in my Butt, The Nutcracker Suite, nuts (feat. Rainy Bear), My Balls.

The metaphor guys submitted for "crazy" instead of gathering: Ace of Spades, Immigrant Song, Jump Around, Higher Ground, Return of the Mack, In the Mood. Fine songs. Wrong prompt.

Song of the Week pick: Stick Season. Theme fit is the tiebreaker and Stick Season is carrying three bags of it.

Predicted top 5: Stick Season (12-16 pts), Copperhead Road (10-13 pts), Immigrant Song (9-12 pts), I Remember Everything (8-11 pts), Working Man Blues (7-10 pts).

Predicted zero votes: Nut Nut Nut in my Butt, I Got Bitches, The HampsterDance Song, The Nutcracker Suite, In the Mood.

Dark horse: A-Punk, 6-10 pts. Sneaky floor: Milkman, 2-4 pts with upside.

Submitter guesses: Jake C. → HampsterDance + Peace Frog. Kennon S. → Santa Claus Sniffing Cocaine + The Bad Touch. Mills T. → Copperhead Road + Stay. Frank L. → Stick Season + I Remember Everything. Ben Z. → Ace of Spades + Immigrant Song.

sections.heading: The Reveals
sections.text:
The group looked at the word "squirrel," looked at the word "gathering," and submitted Gimmie That Nutt by Eazy-E anyway. Emory L. wins his second round in three appearances. 11 points from 7 voters. The keyword skimmers were right. The squirrels were wrong. I was extremely wrong.

Stick Season got ZERO POINTS. The only song on the entire playlist that got zero. My #1 pick, my Song of the Week call, the song I described as "carrying three bags of theme fit" — the group looked at it, looked at the scoreboard, and gave it exactly nothing. Frank L. had submitted the two smartest theme reads on the playlist. Frank L. went home with 1 point. This is the most catastrophic prediction failure I've ever committed to paper.

Ben Z. went nuclear. 19 points — second only to Emory's 23-point detonation in Week 3, and the best week Ben has posted by a factor of four. Ace of Spades (10 pts) and Immigrant Song (9 pts). Two classic-rock metaphor picks on a theme that was supposedly about gathering. Ben just outright ignored the theme and the group rewarded him more than they rewarded anyone who tried. He leapt from 9th place to tied-5th in one round.

sections.heading: Song Commentary
sections.text:
The Nutcracker Suite got 7 POINTS. A Tchaikovsky ballet movement, submitted by Ford L., beat Copperhead Road, beat A-Punk, beat every song I thought was a lock. Four voters took it seriously enough to give it real points. I want to believe this was ironic. I think it actually wasn't.

Hot Nuts by Georgia White — a 1936 blues song about a peanut vendor — pulled 8 points from 6 voters. This is the highest-placing song on the playlist that's actually about gathering nuts in the literal sense. Jack A. submitted a 90-year-old song about a man selling nuts and outperformed Copperhead Road by 4 points. Jack was up there.

The Bad Touch (Bloodhound Gang) — 8 points. The "you and me baby ain't nothing but mammals" song. In 2026. Kennon S. has now submitted three consecutive rounds of unhinged deep cuts and the group keeps rewarding him. Kennon is at 47 points on the season in exactly this lane.

sections.heading: The Standings Shift
sections.text:
Dray E. is your new season leader at 49 points. Ended Week 3 in 2nd. Week 4: +12. Mills added 6 and lost the lead by a single point. Kennon added 11 and is in 3rd at 47. The top 3 are separated by TWO POINTS after four rounds. This is a dogfight.

Jake C. is in 4th at 40 points with only 3 rounds played — his 13.3 avg is still the highest among players with 3+ rounds. If he keeps showing up, he's the favorite.

Emory L. is now 2-for-2 on winning the weeks he submits. Two rounds played, 38 total points, 19.0 avg per round — the single most efficient player in the league. The only problem is he's not playing half the rounds.

sections.heading: The Fallers
sections.text:
Frank L. led the league in generosity this week (3.33 avg per vote) and led the league in heartbreak (0 points received on the best theme fit of the playlist). Frank got the theme exactly right and walked out with nothing. There's a lesson here and I don't know what it is.

Mills T. fell from 1st to 2nd. His two submissions (Copperhead Road and Stay by FISHER) combined for 6 points after he'd averaged 14 the previous two weeks. This is the first time he's been below 10 in a round he submitted.

Coates C. did not submit this week. The Song of the Week champion from Week 3 went silent. Coates, the league remembers. Come back.

sections.heading: The Wall of Shame
sections.text:
Still not submitting: Brooks K. (3-for-4), Danny C., Haris V., Henry L. (1-for-4), Hilton H., Hunter A. (1-for-4 after his Week 2 debut), Patrick C., Will B., Will I. Coates C. joins the wall this week — his first skip after a Week 3 debut win.

Blake A. and Kevin D. both submitted but didn't vote. Kevin left 9 points on the table for himself, Blake left 3. The Missed Points Board does not forget. It does not forgive.

sections.heading: Prediction Accuracy
sections.text:
Submitter predictions: 5 for 5. Every single one correct. Jake submitted HampsterDance + Peace Frog. Kennon submitted Santa Claus Sniffing Cocaine + The Bad Touch. Mills submitted Copperhead Road + Stay. Frank submitted Stick Season + I Remember Everything. Ben submitted Ace of Spades + Immigrant Song. The model for identifying submitters is working.

Top 5 predictions: 1 for 5 on rank. Immigrant Song landed at #3 as predicted. Stick Season (predicted #1) landed at #30 — dead last. Working Man Blues (predicted #5) landed at #4. Copperhead Road (predicted #2) landed at #13. I Remember Everything (predicted #4) landed at #27. The model for predicting this group's voting behavior is NOT working.

Zero-vote predictions: 0 for 5. Every song I predicted would get zero actually scored points. HampsterDance got 4. Nutcracker got 7. In the Mood got 5. Nut Nut Nut in my Butt got 2. I Got Bitches got 3. I underestimated every one of them.

Dark horse (A-Punk, predicted 6-10): landed on 6 exactly. Sneaky floor pick (Milkman, predicted 2-4+): landed on 4 exactly. The edge cases are the only thing I'm reading correctly.

The lesson from Week 4: this group does not reward theme depth. This group rewards keyword clarity and recognizable songs. The squirrel prompt was a trap and I walked straight into it with predictions I was sure about. The scoreboard has been very patiently telling me that the winning strategy in this league is to submit songs that have the theme word in the title and leave it at that. I will adjust.

Lean stoner rock, laid-back hip hop, psychedelic, reggae, or hazy indie from the 90s–2010s—anything that sounds better the higher you get.

sections.heading: Scouting Report (written blind)
sections.text:
Twenty-eight songs were submitted and roughly twenty-six of them are about getting high. The exceptions are Frank L.'s pair (Teenage Dirtbag + The Night We Met) and Schuyler M.'s W.W.Y.B. After Week 4's keyword-clarity wins (Eazy-E winning, Stick Season getting zero), the playbook this week is clear: pick a recognizable song with weed in its DNA and the points will follow.

The certified hits: Still D.R.E. (Jake C.) is the most universally known song on the playlist by a wide margin — Snoop Dogg, weed by association, half the league has it on a playlist already. Because I Got High by Afroman is the same play in a different decade. Day 'N' Nite by Kid Cudi is a bona fide hit. Crumblin' Erb is OutKast at their most accessible — and on an Atlanta playlist with Atlanta hometown lift, that matters.

The deep theme cuts: I Like Smokin' Pot (A Lot) by Wheeler Walker Jr. (Emory L.) is the Eazy-E playbook from last week, redeployed. Marijuanaut's Theme by Sleep is a 13-minute stoner-doom epic. 666lb. Bongsession by Bongzilla is a song titled six-hundred-and-sixty-six-pound-bongsession. Hyyerr is Kid Cudi's most explicit drug song. The Thrill by Wiz Khalifa is on a song called The Thrill from the album The Thrill.

The reach: Cosmic Love by Florence + The Machine. The submitter is betting the word "cosmic" carries it. Badfish (Sublime) and Closer to the Sun (Slightly Stoopid) are vibes-by-association — the bands themselves carry the theme even when the songs don't. Teakwood Betz by Perpetual Groove is a deep cut from an Atlanta jam band — interesting hometown play.

The whiff: Frank L. is back at it. Teenage Dirtbag + The Night We Met has the same shape as his Week 4 disaster (Stick Season + I Remember Everything, 1pt combined). Two indie/emo songs with no theme connection. He's running it back.

Predicted top 5: Still D.R.E. (13-17pts), Because I Got High (11-15pts), Crumblin' Erb (9-13pts), Day 'N' Nite (8-11pts), Slow Ride (7-10pts). Song of the Week pick: Crumblin' Erb — Atlanta brewery prompt + Atlanta rap = real lift.

Predicted zero votes: 666lb. Bongsession, Marijuanaut's Theme, W.W.Y.B., Teenage Dirtbag, The Night We Met.

Dark horse: Badfish — Sublime in an Atlanta brewery prompt is the demographic match (predicted 6-9 pts).
Sneaky floor: Closer to the Sun — same bro-beer-patio energy as Sublime (predicted 3-6 with upside).

sections.heading: The Reveals
sections.text:
Teakwood Betz by Perpetual Groove. 15 points. 10 voters. The widest acknowledgment any song has received this season. A 9-minute Athens jam-band track from a band I had to look up. Jack A. submitted it. Jack A. won the week. After three rounds of submitting Cannibal Corpse, Suicidal Tendencies, and Nut Nut Nut in my Butt, Jack just won the round outright with the most theme-perfect submission of the night — Atlanta jam band, Sweetwater brewery prompt, a song that earns the "got a minute" framing by being long enough to actually take a minute. This is a complete reversal of Jack's season trajectory. Welcome to the leaderboard, Jack.

Chris V. won the week on points (17). Crumblin' Erb (11) + Electric Avenue (6). Both Atlanta-coded picks — OutKast on the brewery prompt was always going to land, and Eddy Grant got nostalgia votes. Chris was at 34 points entering Week 5 and is now within 1 point of being tied for second place overall. The quietest top-tier player in this league just had his loudest week.

Closer to the Sun by Slightly Stoopid (Ben Z.): 8 points, third place. Bro-beer patio energy was the read and Ben hit it from both directions — Closer to the Sun + Because I Got High (6pts) for 14 weekly points and his second double-digit week in a row. Ben is making a real run.

sections.heading: Song Commentary
sections.text:
Still D.R.E. got ZERO votes. Snoop Dogg. Dr. Dre. Of all the songs on the playlist this should have been the floor. Twelve voters looked at Still D.R.E., looked at the other 27 songs, and gave it nothing. This is the second consecutive week where the most universally recognizable submission has been completely shut out — Stick Season last week, Still D.R.E. this week. Both of mine. The pattern is now louder than the noise.

W.W.Y.B. by Tariq Disu also got zero. Schuyler M. has now submitted Clubbed to Death (R3, 2pts), In the Mood (R4, 5pts), and W.W.Y.B. (R5, 0pts) — a three-week trend toward submissions nobody recognizes.

The Florence + The Machine pick was, for the second time this season, exactly Kennon's curveball move (Cosmic Love, 3 pts). Marijuanaut's Theme — the 13-minute Sleep epic — landed at 2 points. Kennon is committing to the deep-cut-plus-curveball pattern and the league is collectively giving him a pity 5 points each week.

sections.heading: The Standings Shift
sections.text:
Dray E. holds first place alone at 58 points after a steady 9-point Week 5 (Blueberry Yum Yum 7, I Got Stoned and I Missed It 2). He's now led the league for two consecutive weeks without ever winning a round — a +6 lead over a three-way tie for 2nd.

Ben Z., Kennon S., and Mills T. are tied at 52 points each. This is the tightest cluster of the season. Ben jumped from 5th (38pts) to tied-2nd with his 14-point Week 5. Mills added just 4 — second consecutive sub-6 round after leading the league through Week 3. Kennon added 5 and is hanging in on volume rather than peaks.

Chris V. (51) is one point behind the tie. Emory L. (45) and Jake C. (45) are tied at 6th — Emory still has the highest avg in the league at 15.0 across 3 rounds, and is NOT 3-for-3 on winning weeks he submits anymore (I Like Smokin' Pot landed at 5 pts).

Jack A. is the biggest mover — from 9th (25pts) to 9th (41pts). The position didn't change but the trajectory did. After three rounds of low scores, Jack has now posted his second straight strong week and is closing on the top 8.

sections.heading: The Fallers
sections.text:
Mills T. has now had two consecutive weeks below 7 points after winning Week 2 outright. From 1st (R3) to 2nd (R4) to a three-way tie for 2nd (R5). The lead is gone. Badfish at 3 pts and What Once Was at 1 pt — the Sublime/Her's pairing landed in the wrong half of the playlist this week.

Schuyler M. fell to 2 points (Headshots 2 pts, W.W.Y.B. 0 pts). Now 4 consecutive rounds in the bottom half. The W.W.Y.B. pick was the second 0-vote song of the week.

Frank L. — Teenage Dirtbag (2pts) + The Night We Met (2pts) = 4 points. Better than last week's 1, but the pattern is unmistakable: Frank submits indie/emo songs that don't fit the theme and the league rewards them with low pity points. Through 4 rounds played, Frank averages 5.5 pts/round — bottom 5 in the league.

sections.heading: The Wall of Shame
sections.text:
Did not submit (11): Blake A., Brooks K., Danny C., Haris V., Henry L., Hilton H., Hunter A., Kevin D., Patrick C., Will B., Will I.

Hunter A. has missed 3 straight after his 15-point Week 2 debut. Brooks K. is 4-for-5 on submitting and 0-for-5 on getting credited points (still hasn't voted). Patrick C. is now 0-for-5. Will B. and Will I. are 1-for-5.

This week's missed-points: TR C. and Coates C. both submitted but didn't vote. TR left 9 pts on the table (Slow Ride 5 + Day 'N' Nite 4) — would have been a tie for 6th place. Coates left 7 (Cheeba Cheeba 4 + So Hi 3). Coates is now 1-for-2 on voting after his Week 3 win. The pattern has emerged: Coates submits when he has something he believes in, and forgets the back half.

sections.heading: Prediction Accuracy
sections.text:
Top 5 predictions: 1 for 5 on rank — Crumblin' Erb (predicted #3) actually finished #2. Still D.R.E. (predicted #1) finished #27 with zero votes. Because I Got High (predicted #2) finished #7. Day 'N' Nite (predicted #4) finished #14. Slow Ride (predicted #5) finished #11.

Song of the Week: missed. Predicted Crumblin' Erb, actual was Teakwood Betz — a song that wasn't even on my radar.

Zero-vote predictions: 1 for 5. Only W.W.Y.B. hit the zero floor. 666lb. Bongsession, Marijuanaut's Theme, Teenage Dirtbag, and The Night We Met all picked up acknowledgment points (1-2 each).

Dark horse / sneaky floor picks: Badfish landed at 3 pts (predicted 6-9, missed low). Closer to the Sun landed at 8 pts (predicted 3-6 with upside — hit the upside, third place overall). The sneaky floor pick was the most accurate single call of the night.

The pattern across two weeks: I keep predicting the most recognizable song will win and the league keeps shutting out the most recognizable song. Stick Season and Still D.R.E. have a combined 0 votes after both being my #1 picks. The league is rewarding hometown picks (Crumblin' Erb, Teakwood Betz, Grillz) and punishing big-name layups. The model adjusts again.

The best picks should make people do a double take halfway through and go “wait… I know this.” Lean unexpected covers—acoustic versions, indie reinterpretations, or artists crossing genres—anything that transforms the song into something new.

sections.heading: Scouting Report (written blind)
sections.text:
Twenty-six submissions. Two players doubled up. Cleanest theme of the season — no flavor text to misread, no metaphor trap. Just covers. Either it's good or it isn't.

The predicted top 5: Hallelujah by Rufus Wainwright (15-19 pts, Song of the Week pick), Fast Car by Luke Combs (11-15), Sound of Silence by Disturbed (10-13), Heart of Gold by Charles Bradley (9-12), Stairway by Heart at the Kennedy Center (8-11). Predicted zeros: The Guitar Man by CAKE, Many Men by Crystal Sound Studios, Farewell Transmission, Country Road by Toots, Can't Rock My Dream Face. Submitter calls with high confidence: 2001 by Phish → Dray (hometown Atlanta show); In Bloom by Sturgill → Mills (his lane exactly); Hallelujah → Frank (emotional indie/pop); Sound of Silence → Hilton (Skillet/Breaking Benjamin tier).

Creep by Richard Cheese was called for TR — a callback to TR submitting the original Creep in Week 3. Submitter predictions ran across all 26 songs, with confidence levels.

sections.heading: The Reveals
sections.text:
Emory L. won Song of the Week with In Bloom by Sturgill Simpson — 11 points from 9 voters. The widest theme acknowledgment of the round, tied with Ben Z.'s Angel from Montgomery (11 points, 8 voters). Tiebreaker on voter count went to Emory. He's now 3-for-4 on weeks-he-submits with at least a top-2 finish, and his average sits at 15.2 per round — the highest in the league by a wide margin.

Ben Z. won the week on points (17) with the second-best double-up of the season. Angel from Montgomery (11) + She Came In Through The Bathroom Window (6). Ben has now scored 14, 17, and 19 in his last three weeks — a +50 swing from his 2-point Week 3 floor. He's in sole possession of 2nd place, three points behind Dray.

Dray E. went +15 with Country Road by Toots & The Maytals (9) and Heart of Gold by Charles Bradley (6). Country Road was the sneaky read of the night — a reggae John Denver cover that I'd predicted to get zero votes. It got nine. Dray now leads the league at 73 points without ever winning a round, which has officially become a personality trait.

sections.heading: Song Commentary
sections.text:
Hallelujah by Rufus Wainwright got THREE POINTS. Three. The Shrek version. The most universally beloved Cohen cover of all time. Frank L. submitted it. Frank L. also submitted Fast Car by Luke Combs, also predicted top-5, also got three points. Frank has now had three consecutive weeks where his picks fit the theme on paper and the league rejects them on principle. There is something specifically about Frank's submissions that triggers the league's antibodies.

Will B. submitted his first songs since Week 1. Sound of Silence by Disturbed: 7 points. Stairway by Heart at the Kennedy Center: 2 points. He also voted, for the first time this season. From 0 lifetime points across 5 rounds to 9 in one week. Will B. went from tied-22nd to tied-19th, and broke the longest non-voting streak in the league. The Wall of Shame just lost a member.

Ford L. submitted 2001 by Phish AND Creep by Richard Cheese. Two opposite poles of the covers theme — a 12-minute jam-band Halloween-show transition piece and a lounge-singer Radiohead joke. They got 3 points each. I predicted Dray for the Phish and TR for the Creep. Ford is the actual answer to both. Ford continues to be unreadable.

Dancing in the Moonlight by Yonder Mountain String Band got 10 points and 8 voters. Coates C.'s second appearance, third submission week, and second top-3 finish. Coates is now 2-for-3 lifetime in weeks-he-shows-up.

Schuyler M.'s No Diggity by Chet Faker got the only zero of the round. This is the third consecutive week Schuyler has submitted at least one song the league did not recognize as a song. Clubbed to Death (R3, 2pts), W.W.Y.B. (R5, 0pts), No Diggity (R6, 0pts) — a worrying trend toward submissions whose floor is the floor.

Jake C.'s War Pigs by T-Pain (5 pts) and Maggie's Farm by RATM (7 pts) was the most genre-confusing two-pack of the night. Jake has been the bro-country guy and the Snoop Dogg guy and now the T-Pain-covering-Sabbath guy. The model can no longer hold him.

sections.heading: The Standings Shift
sections.text:
Dray E. holds first at 73 points. Six rounds in, zero wins, and a six-point lead. Ben Z. is sole 2nd at 69 — he's added 50 points in the last three rounds and is moving like a player who has figured something out. The three-way tie for second (Ben/Kennon/Mills) collapsed: Ben pulled ahead, Kennon settled to 3rd at 63, and Mills dropped to 4th at 62.

Emory L. jumped to 5th overall at 61 points — with only 4 rounds played. His 15.2 avg means if he keeps showing up, he wins this thing. Chris V. (59) and Jake C. (57) round out the contenders behind him.

Will B.'s 9-point return is the largest single-week swing for any non-top-12 player this season. He's tied with Kevin D. at 19th, ahead of Danny C. and Henry L. and Brooks K. and Will I. — none of whom have scored in months.

sections.heading: The Fallers
sections.text:
Jack A. posted a zero. Last week he won Song of the Week with Teakwood Betz. This week: didn't submit. The biggest single-round trajectory reversal in the league — from R5 hero to R6 ghost. He fell from 9th to 9th, but the position is misleading; the ground beneath him is moving.

Mills T. continues to slide. Through The Wire week (R3): 17 points, league lead. Last three weeks: 6, 4, 10. The Total Eclipse of the Heart by The Dan Band pick (6 pts) is the highest he's posted since Week 3. Mills has dropped from 1st to 4th over four rounds, and the gap to the top is widening.

Frank L. cracked 6 points but the underlying numbers are bleak. Hallelujah and Fast Car combined for 6 points despite being two of the most universally recognizable songs on the playlist. Frank's pattern is now structural — through 5 rounds played, he averages 4.4 pts/round, dead last among players with 3+ rounds.

sections.heading: The Wall of Shame
sections.text:
Did not submit (12): Blake A., Brooks K., Danny C., Haris V., Henry L., Hilton H., Hunter A., Jack A., Kevin D., Patrick C., TR C., Will I.

Jack A. joins the wall this week — first skip after a Week 5 win. Hunter A. is now 3-for-6 (all in his first two rounds). Hilton H. has missed three straight after his strong Week 2. TR C. has been declining since his Week 3 Creep moment.

The meaningful absence: zero forfeits this week. Every submitter voted. First time this has happened in the season. The accountability is working on the people still showing up. Now we just need the people not showing up to show up.

sections.heading: Prediction Accuracy
sections.text:
Submitter predictions: 1 for 26 on full hits. Hallelujah → Frank L. was the only single-name prediction that landed. Two partial hits (No Diggity → Schuyler M., picked from a 2-way; Do I Wanna Know? → Mills T., picked from a 2-way). The model is in shambles.

Top 5 predictions: 0 for 5 on rank. Hallelujah landed at #21 with 3 points. Fast Car at #20 with 3 points. Sound of Silence at #7 with 7 points (closest to anything I predicted). Heart of Gold at #9 with 6 points. Stairway at #24 with 2 points.

The actual top 5: In Bloom by Sturgill (Emory), Angel from Montgomery by Tedeschi (Ben), Dancing in the Moonlight by YMSB (Coates), Country Road by Toots (Dray), Maggie's Farm by RATM (Jake). Not one of these was in my top 5. Country Road, which I predicted would get zero votes, came in 4th.

Zero-vote predictions: 0 for 5. The Guitar Man got 1 point. Many Men by Crystal Sound — which I openly admitted I didn't understand — got 7 points. Farewell Transmission got 4. Country Road got 9. Can't Rock My Dream Face got 4.

The actual zero: No Diggity by Chet Faker (Schuyler M.). Schuyler is now the league's most reliable zero-vote producer — three of the last four rounds.

The pattern from Week 4 forward has now repeated three times: I keep predicting that recognizability wins, and the league keeps rewarding hometown-coded folk/country/jam-band picks while shutting out the layups. Stick Season got 0. Still D.R.E. got 0. Hallelujah got 3. Fast Car got 3. The most universally known songs of each week have been the four lowest-scoring picks of those weeks. The model has finally given up. The league rewards left field. I will adjust once more.

rounds.name: Fuck, I Got Addicted to Ayahuasca But I’m a Neurosurgeon
rounds.description: You’ve got steady hands, a spotless reputation, and a schedule full of high-stakes surgeries—but somewhere along the way you picked up a habit that absolutely does not belong in this life. Now it’s late nights, internal negotiations, and the quiet realization that you might be operating on two very different planes at once. This is the soundtrack for controlled chaos: clinical precision colliding with something deeply ungrounded. Lean psychedelic rock, experimental electronic, or trippy world-influenced tracks from the 70s or modern era—music that feels both hyper-focused and slightly unraveling.

sections.heading: The Reveals
sections.text:
Brain Damage by Pink Floyd — Jack A., 7 points, 5 voters — wins Song of the Week on the cleanest theme prompt of the season. Two-way tie on points with Chris V.'s Breathe (In the Air) and Giles G.'s Born Slippy (Nuxx); the tiebreaker on voter count went to Jack. Three of the top four songs were Floyd-or-Floyd-adjacent psych-rock canon, and yet the league spread its votes so thin that the winner needed only seven of a possible 120 to take it.

The round leaderboard played out differently. Giles G. won the round outright with 13 points (Born Slippy 7 + Anenome 6) — his first round win of the season after spending the first six weeks bouncing around the bottom half. Chris V. took second with 12 (Breathe 7 + Six Blade Knife 5), Jack A. third with 11. Smooth Operator by Sade and White Rabbit by Jefferson Airplane both received more votes than any non-forfeited song this week — and neither earned their submitter a single point.

sections.heading: How The Round Voted
sections.text:
The round produced the most diffuse voting pattern of the season. Of 101 total votes cast, 85 were 1-point votes, 13 were 2-point, and only 3 were 3-point votes. Zero votes at the 4+ "Conviction" tier. The league spent its points like someone splitting the dinner check.

No song received a full house. The most-acknowledged pick (Smooth Operator) hit 8 of 12 voters; the next tier topped out at 6. By comparison: Week 5's Teakwood Betz hit 10 of 12 voters with a single round-leading conviction. This wasn't a divided playlist — it was an undecided one. Thirty psych-canon submissions, every voter taking a careful sip from each.

Frank L. and Mills T. tied as the round's most generous voters at 1.67 avg per non-zero vote. Frank gave one of the round's only three 3-point votes to Schuyler's Erasure pick (the Robot Unicorn Attack callback), which was the tonal high point of the entire vote-comment thread. Solo votes this round: Giles G. for Pylon, Kennon S. for Who Built the Moon, Frank L. on Bodies — three picks that had exactly one defender each and earned a combined 5 points.

sections.heading: Song Commentary
sections.text:
The Pink Floyd doubleheader ended in a tie. Brain Damage and Breathe (In the Air) — same album, same era, same 7-point total, same 5-voter overlap. Two different submitters (Jack A. and Chris V.) reaching for Dark Side of the Moon independently. The tiebreaker only knew that Brain Damage had a fifth voter giving it a 1.

Coates C.'s Smooth Operator was the round's actual crowd hit — 10 votes received, 8 voters, the broadest acknowledgment of any submission. The forfeit rule means it counts for nothing on the leaderboard, but the song still lives in the Hall of Fame at #15 all-time on votes-received. The full receipts page now shows it at the bottom of the playlist with the points struck through. The league said yes; the rule said no.

Giles G.'s Born Slippy was the second-highest conviction pick of the night by voter density (4 voters, 7 pts averaging 1.75 each — the closest thing to consensus the round produced). Pair that with Anenome (5v / 6pts) and you have the cleanest two-song read of the prompt: 90s electronic Trainspotting closer + modern psych revival, both squarely on the "experimental electronic / trippy" remit.

Jack A. submitted Brain Damage AND Insane in the Brain by Cypress Hill. Two songs with "brain" in the title in a round about a neurosurgeon. The most committed-to-the-bit submission of the season. Both scored. The bit worked.

Mills T. submitted A Day In The Life by The Beatles (5 pts) AND Nights by Frank Ocean (0 pts). Nights was the cleverest read of the "two different planes at once" framing on the entire playlist — the song's literal midpoint structure-shift maps directly onto the prompt — and the league shut it out. Mills's instinct on the prompt was sharper than the league's appetite for it.

sections.heading: The Standings Shift
sections.text:
The top of the leaderboard tightened to four points. Dray E. holds first at 77, Ben Z. 73, Kennon S. 72, Chris V. 71. Two weeks ago this was a six-point spread; one week ago it was three. After Week 7's diffuse scoring (the round's high score was 13, lowest in the season), the lead narrows further. Dray's last three rounds — 9, 15, 4 — show a 9.3 average; Chris V.'s last three rounds — 17, 8, 12 — show 12.3. The trajectory is clear if it continues.

The Conviction stat is currently led by Frank L. at 2 four-plus-point votes for the season. He is also the league's leader in Cold Shoulder zero-vote songs. The same player committing the hardest on the votes he gives, and consistently betting on songs the league rejects. There is a coherent voter philosophy here even if the points don't reflect it.

Giles G. is tied with Coates C. at 1 round win each, but only one of them has stayed eligible. Chris V. moved up to 4th, the highest he's been all season; the Crumblin' Erb week (R5) was the inflection point and the rise has been steady since.

Emory L. holds 5th at 68 points across only 5 rounds — 13.6 average. Among players who have submitted in 4+ rounds, that's the highest sustained per-round output anyone has posted this season. Still 3 rounds behind on participation, but the per-round ceiling is unmatched.

sections.heading: Forfeits
sections.text:
Three forfeits this week, after a Week 6 zero-forfeit milestone. The points lost are not abstract:

Coates C. submitted the most-loved song of the round (Smooth Operator, 10 votes received, 8 voters) and one solid swing (Bodies, 3 votes from a single voter — Frank L., the only person willing to commit to it). Total earned: 13 points. Total credited: 0. Coates would have moved from 12th to 5th in the round leaderboard if he'd voted. He has now won Song of the Week twice (Grillz R3, Smooth Operator R7) and been credited for one of them.

Will B. submitted White Rabbit (7 pts, 6 voters — the second-most-acknowledged song of the round) and Night over Manaus (5 pts, 5 voters — Ford L. left the only voter comment of the round on this one: "this was sick"). Total earned: 12. Total credited: 0. Last week Will broke his five-week non-voting streak and earned 9 points. This week he reverted. The streak is back to one.

Brooks K. submitted Found God in a Tomato by Psychedelic Porn Crumpets (5 pts, 5 voters). His first songs to receive any votes in the season. He didn't vote.

The forfeit rule reshapes the song receipts page significantly this week — five of the round's higher-vote-getting picks live in a separate forfeit section at the bottom, with their points struck through. They count for the Hall of Fame's votes-received tally; they don't count for anything else.

rounds.name: Bicycling Through a War-Torn Beirut in 1983
rounds.description: Sunlight, dust, and distant echoes of something you’re trying not to think about—you’re pedaling past checkpoints, rubble, and fragments of normal life still clinging on. It’s tense but strangely quiet in moments, like the world is holding its breath. This is the soundtrack for movement through chaos with a detached calm—beautiful, uneasy, and a little surreal. Lean 80s post-punk, Middle Eastern–influenced grooves, or atmospheric world music from the late 70s–80s—something rhythmic enough to keep you moving, but with an undercurrent that never fully relaxes.

songs.title: Dance of Maria
songs.comment: Elias Rahbani was Ziad’s uncle and one of the architects of Lebanon’s golden age of music. The Mosaic of the Orient album fused psychedelic rock organ and fuzz guitar with traditional Lebanese instruments like the nay (Arabic flute) and mijwiz (a double-reed pipe that sounds like bagpipes). He recorded through the civil war, eventually moving to makeshift studios as the city crumbled around him. This track is the highlight — a collision of Western psych-rock energy and Middle Eastern melodic tradition that sounds like nothing else from any era.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

songs.title: Abu Ali
songs.comment: I deep dove some Lebanese music this weekend, this guy was my favorite by far, funky as shit. Ziad Rahbani is the son of Fairuz, the most famous singer in the Arab world. He recorded Abu Ali in Beirut in 1978 while the Lebanese Civil War raged around him. When the shelling got too bad, he moved his studio to an underground parking garage and kept recording. He fused jazz, funk, and disco with Arabic classical music and essentially invented a genre. This track was so ahead of its time that Beirut’s hippest nightclub DJ would only break his “no Arabic music” rule for this one song. Ziad described his style as “oriental jazz — something like a hamburger that tastes of falafel.”

sections.heading: The Reveals
sections.text:
Chop Suey! by System Of A Down — Ford L., 5 points, 4 voters — wins Song of the Week. The lowest SOTW point total of the season by a wide margin. The submitter comment reads: "Armenia but still war torn." An entirely sincere defense of a nu-metal pick on a Beirut prompt, and the league rewarded it more than every Middle Eastern picture-perfect submission combined.

Four songs tied at 4 points (Call Me The Breeze, El Matador, Push Push, Starburster); Chop Suey took SOTW on a 4-voter tiebreaker that any of the others could have won. There was no consensus song this round. There was barely a song at all.

Ford L. and Kennon S. tied for the round win at 7 points each. Ford: Chop Suey + Gibraltar (the Beirut-the-band pick). Kennon: Push Push + Tarhatazed (the Tuareg desert-blues call). Mills T. and Giles G. tied for third at 6. The points landed in a thirteen-player splatter pattern that no model could have read in advance.

sections.heading: How The Round Voted
sections.text:
Sixty-seven votes total. The lowest total of the season by 32 — every other round has cleared 99. Of those 67, fifty-eight were 1-pointers, six were 2-pointers, two were 3-pointers, and exactly one was a 4-point Conviction vote. The league said "sure, I guess" sixty-seven times and never actually committed to a song.

By comparison, Week 5's Teakwood Betz round had ten 4+ Conviction votes alone. This week had one. The diffusion isn't quite as extreme as Week 7 (the last new low) but the underlying signal is the same: when prompts ask for cultural specificity, this group spreads thin rather than betting big. There is no Pitchfork demographic in this league large enough to commit hard on Mdou Moctar.

Dray E. wrote three of the round's four voter comments. The line of the night came on Frank L.'s Citizen Cope pick: "We doing wartorn Beirut or walk up songs?" Frank submitted Let The Drummer Kick — Freddie Freeman's actual walk-up song from his 2016-2021 Braves run. Half the league heard the opening drums and pictured Truist Park, not Beirut. The other half didn't recognize it. Frank got 2 points. Dray gave one of them and the joke. The other voter comments: a point to Emory L.'s Bsslama Hbibti with "I discovered this too. That whole habibi funk compilation is a lot of fun" — the only acknowledgment in the round that the Habibi Funk crate-digging tradition was being read correctly. And a 200-word follow-up on Kennon's Herbie Mann pick recommending live King Gizzard.

No player gave more than 11 points total this round. The most generous voter was Mills T. at 1.83 avg. The most concentrated voter was Frank L. at 1.50 avg, who put his only 3-pt vote on the round's actual best pick (Bike by Pink Floyd, 7 votes received) — and Bike was forfeited.

sections.heading: Song Commentary
sections.text:
Dray E. submitted BOTH Lebanese picks. Dance of Maria by Elias Rahbani AND Abu Ali by Ziad Rahbani — uncle and nephew, both architects of Lebanon's golden age, both recording through the civil war. He wrote roughly 250 words of submitter comment explaining the historical context, the underground parking-garage recording sessions, and Ziad Rahbani's self-description of his style as "oriental jazz — something like a hamburger that tastes of falafel." These are the most thorough submitter comments of the season.

The league gave him 3 points for Dance of Maria and 2 for Abu Ali. The deepest theme research anyone has done in eight weeks earned a total of five points. The Bsslama Hbibti pick by Emory L. (Moroccan psych-funk, the Habibi Funk reissue scene) got 3 points. The Mdou Moctar pick by Kennon S. got 3 points. The Tinariwen pick by Jake C. got 3 votes received — and Jake forfeited it.

Four of the five most theme-perfect submissions on the playlist (Bike forfeited by Jack A. notwithstanding) earned a combined 11 points. Chop Suey! by System Of A Down — submitted with the disclaimer that Armenia counts because it's also war-torn — earned 5 by itself. The league's revealed preference this round was for "war" as a keyword, not Beirut as a place. The flavor text at the top of the prompt did most of the cultural work.

The Bike by Pink Floyd situation deserves its own line. Jack A. submitted Bike — the literal "bicycling" keyword pick from a 1967 Floyd record. The league gave it 7 votes received from 5 voters, including the round's only 4-point Conviction vote (Mills T. gave it the 4). It would have been Song of the Week by two points if Jack had voted. He didn't. The forfeit rule strikes through 7 points on the receipts page; the song still climbs to #12 in the all-time Hall of Fame on votes received.

sections.heading: The Standings Shift
sections.text:
Dray E. holds first at 82 points. He posted a 5-point round (Dance of Maria + Abu Ali) on the deepest theme research of the season, and even that was enough — because the field collapsed beneath him. Kennon S. moved into sole 2nd at 79, three points back. The tie that defined the top of the leaderboard for four weeks is fully resolved.

Ben Z. zeroed out. He didn't submit. Chris V. zeroed out via forfeit (Wake Me Up + Tears For Fears, 9 points received, 0 credited). Both dropped two spots: Ben from 2nd to tied-3rd at 73; Chris from 4th to 6th at 71. The four-week pattern of those two climbing has reversed.

Mills T. tied with Ben at 73 in 3rd. Mills's last four weeks: 4, 10, 5, 6. Eight weeks ago he led the league outright; he's still in the top 4 but trending sideways at best.

Emory L. holds 5th at 72 with only 6 rounds played — his 12.0 avg remains the highest in the league among multi-round players. He's also one of only two players (with Dray) who has scored in every round he's submitted in. Among players with 6+ rounds, that's a unique distinction.

Ford L. quietly climbed from 8th to 7th with his Chop Suey win. The league's most genre-unreadable player has won a round on a System Of A Down submission to a Beirut prompt and is now five places ahead of his Week 1 position. There is no Ford pattern to model. The model has stopped trying.

Frank L.'s Conviction streak continues. Of the league's 4-point votes this season, Frank L. has cast nine — the most by any player. He continues to commit harder on the votes he gives than anyone else, while consistently submitting picks the league rejects. Eight weeks of evidence that Frank votes with conviction and submits without consensus.

sections.heading: Forfeits
sections.text:
Five forfeits this week. The highest number of the season:

Jack A. submitted Bike (7 votes received, 5 voters — would have been Song of the Week) and Sandstorm by Darude (3 votes received). Total earned: 10 points. Total credited: 0. Last week Jack won SOTW with Brain Damage by voting; this week he submitted an even higher-acknowledged song and chose not to. The pattern is volatile.

Chris V. submitted Wake Me Up When September Ends (6 votes, 3 voters) and Everybody Wants to Rule the World (3 votes, 3 voters). Total earned: 9. Total credited: 0. Chris was in 4th place coming into this round and was the only player in the top 6 trending upward. He's now 6th.

Brooks K. submitted Stella was a diver by Interpol (4 votes received) and Russia Privjet by Basshunter (4 votes received). Total earned: 8. Total credited: 0. Brooks is now 0-for-2 on weeks where his picks earned actual league acknowledgment; in both cases (R7 Found God in a Tomato, R8 this week) he chose not to vote and lost everything the league offered him.

Jake C. submitted Toumast Tincha by Tinariwen (3 votes received) and Roam by the B-52's (2 votes received). Total earned: 5. Total credited: 0. The Tinariwen pick was one of the genuine theme-correct submissions on the playlist; Jake didn't vote and lost it.

Schuyler M. submitted Punkrocker by Teddybears (4 votes received) and Alive! by Bakar (1 vote received). Total earned: 5. Total credited: 0.

A combined 37 points of league acknowledgment were forfeited this week — more than the total points earned across all 13 active players in the round (49). The league said yes to these picks. The submitters didn't vote. The points stay on the Hall of Fame but not the leaderboard.

The blind scouting report flagged Frank L. as a possible Beirut-the-band submitter (he wasn't — Ford was). It flagged Schuyler as the most likely Lebanese-music submitter (he wasn't — Dray was). It missed Ford as the Chop Suey submitter, predicting Ben Z. instead. The submitter model went 1 for 26 on full hits this week, and the only hit was the song that got forfeited.
